audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 2 Samuel chapter 10, first-pass draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for diplomatic condolence, suspicion of espionage, beard and garment humiliation, masculinity and sexual shame, mercenary armies, escalation, casualty figures, vassalage, and Divine-war rhetoric.

verses.restored: After this, king of children of Ammon died, and Hanun his son reigned in his place.

David said, I will do covenant-kindness with Hanun son of Nahash, as his father did kindness with me. David sent by hand of his servants to comfort him concerning his father. David's servants came into land of children of Ammon.

Officials of children of Ammon said to Hanun their lord, Is David honoring your father in your eyes by sending comforters? Has David not sent his servants to search city, spy it out, and overthrow it?
Hanun took David's servants, shaved off half of each beard, cut their garments in middle as far as buttocks, and sent them away.
They told David, and he sent to meet them, because men were greatly humiliated. King said, Stay at Jericho until your beards grow, then return.

Children of Ammon saw that they had made themselves odious to David. They sent and hired Arameans of Beth-rehob and Zobah, twenty thousand foot soldiers, king of Maacah with one thousand, and men of Tob, twelve thousand.
David heard and sent Joab with all army of mighty warriors.
Children of Ammon came out and arranged battle at entrance of gate. Arameans of Zobah and Rehob, men of Tob, and Maacah were by themselves in field.
Joab saw face of battle was against him in front and behind. He chose from all chosen men of Israel and arranged them against Arameans.
Rest of people he placed in hand of Abishai his brother, who arranged them against children of Ammon.
He said, If Arameans are stronger than I, you shall rescue me; if children of Ammon are stronger than you, I will go to rescue you.
Be strong, and let us strengthen ourselves for our people and for cities of our Divine. May YHWH do what is good in YHWH's eyes.
Joab and people with him drew near to battle against Arameans, and they fled before him.
Children of Ammon saw Arameans had fled, and they also fled before Abishai and entered city. Joab returned from children of Ammon and came to Jerusalem.
Arameans saw they were defeated before Israel and gathered themselves together.
Hadadezer sent and brought out Arameans from beyond River. They came to Helam, with Shobach commander of Hadadezer's army before them.
It was told David. He gathered all Israel, crossed Jordan, and came to Helam. Arameans arranged themselves against David and fought with him.
Arameans fled before Israel. David killed from Arameans seven hundred chariot crews and forty thousand horsemen/foot soldiers; he struck Shobach commander of army, and he died there.
All kings who were servants of Hadadezer saw they were defeated before Israel. They made peace with Israel and served them. Arameans feared to rescue children of Ammon again.

translation_notes: The initial mission is diplomatic condolence; suspicion turns it into humiliation and war. Misread intentions do not make escalation inevitable.
Half-shaved beards and cut garments expose bodies and attack masculine honor. The men's shame belongs to coercive humiliation, not to their bodies, hair, nudity, sexuality, or gender expression.
The phrase that Ammon 'became odious' to David is political perception, not proof that an entire people became morally repulsive.
Hired forces and royal coalitions show war as organized labor and finance, not a simple contest of ethnic heroes.
Joab's mutual-rescue plan has tactical solidarity, but courage language must not recruit people into avoidable war or equate masculinity with violence.
Casualty numbers and the horsemen/foot-soldier textual variation require witness audit. Every number represents human death.
The final peace produces service/vassalage under Israel. It is not reconciliation between equals and should not be celebrated as benevolent empire.
safety_note: Do not use bodily humiliation to enforce masculinity, mock exposed people, or justify war. Do not turn Ammonites or Arameans into timeless enemies. The chapter records diplomatic failure, retaliation, mercenary escalation, mass death, and coerced political submission.
